topic: village football match — coaching youth team

turns.content: we have a football match next Saturday. our village youth team against Adomfe town. I am coaching these boys my friend but they do not listen. can you give me some simple tactics for a team that is fast but not strong

If your boys are fast but not strong, you want to avoid physical battles and use speed as your weapon. Here are some simple tactics:

1. **Play wide and fast.** Keep the ball on the wings where your quick players can run past defenders. Don't go through the middle where bigger players can muscle you off.

2. **Counter-attack.** Let Adomfe come forward, then hit them on the break. Fast players are deadly in open space. Defend as a group, win the ball, then one quick pass to a runner.

3. **Short passing.** Don't boot long balls — stronger players win those. Keep the ball on the ground with quick one-two passes.

4. **Press high early.** In the first 15 minutes, use your energy and speed to pressure their defenders. Score early if you can.

How many players do you have? And what is the pitch like — grass, dirt?
dirt pitch. very hard ground now because no rain for weeks. we play 11 aside. I have 14 boys so 3 substitutes. the Adomfe boys are bigger. they play rough my friend

Hard dirt pitch is actually good for your style. The ball moves fast on hard ground, which favours quick passing and speed. Bigger players struggle more on hard surfaces because it's harder on their legs.

For dealing with rough play:

- Tell your boys **not to react.** If Adomfe plays rough, let the referee handle it. Reacting gets your players sent off or injured.
- **Don't hold the ball too long.** One touch, two touch maximum. If your player receives the ball and passes quickly, the big defenders can't get a tackle in.
- **Use your 3 substitutes wisely.** Bring on fresh legs around the 55-60 minute mark. If your boys are fast now, imagine how fast they look when Adomfe's big players are tired.

Who is your best player? Where does he play?
Emmanuel. left side. very fast. he can run past anyone but sometimes he runs into trouble. tries to beat 3 men instead of passing

Every team has an Emmanuel! Fast and brave but wants to do it all himself.

Here's what to tell him:

- **"Beat one, pass."** Make it a rule. He can dribble past the first defender — that's his strength. But as soon as he sees the second defender coming, he must pass. Simple rule, easy to remember.
- Put a **runner on his side** — someone who overlaps and gives him an option. When Emmanuel beats his man and looks up, he should see a teammate running ahead of him.
- Tell Emmanuel that the **assist is as important as the goal.** If he creates a goal with a pass, the whole village will cheer just as loud.

What about your goalkeeper? Is he reliable?
the goalkeeper is okay. not tall but he is brave. he comes out for crosses which is good. my worry is the defence. the centre backs are young. 15 years. they get nervous

Young centre backs getting nervous is normal. Here's how to help them:

- **Give them one simple instruction each.** Don't overload them with tactics. One boy marks the striker, the other covers behind. That's it. Clear jobs.
- **Talk, talk, talk.** Tell them to communicate constantly. When young defenders talk to each other — "mine," "behind you," "push up" — they feel less alone and make fewer mistakes.
- If you have **one older or calmer player**, put him in midfield just in front of the defence. He protects the young boys and sweeps up loose balls before trouble reaches them.
- **Don't shout at them from the sideline when they make a mistake.** They already know. Encourage them. A calm coach makes calm defenders.

At 15, they're learning. This match is their classroom.
yes that is wise. I was shouting too much last match. hah. my friend the problem is I care too much. when the boy makes a mistake I feel it in my chest

That's because you're a real coach — you care about the boys, not just the result. But they can feel your tension from the sideline. If the coach is stressed, the players are stressed.

Try this on Saturday:

- Before the match, tell the boys your plan calmly. Make them repeat the key points back to you.
- During the match, **only shout encouragement and simple instructions.** "Wide!" "Pass!" "Well done!" Nothing complicated.
- At halftime, two things only: one thing they did well, one thing to improve. No more.

Your boys will play better when they feel trusted. A farmer who shouts at his trees does not make them grow faster.

hah. a farmer who shouts at his trees. that is a good one my friend. I will remember that on Saturday. okay so the plan is play wide, counter attack, Emmanuel beats one and passes, centre backs talk and stay calm
You've got it. That's the whole plan and it's simple enough for every boy to remember. Write it down on a piece of paper and give it to your captain too. Let the boys own the plan.
the captain is Bright. good boy. calm head. he can explain it to the others before the match. what about set pieces my friend. corners and free kicks. we always waste them

Set pieces are where smaller teams can score against bigger ones. Keep it very simple:

**Corners:** Pick your two tallest players and put them near the goal. One at the near post, one at the far post. The corner taker delivers the ball to the near post — if the near post player can't reach it, it goes through to the far post. Practice this 5 times before Saturday and it becomes automatic.

**Free kicks:** If close to goal, one shot on target. Not fancy — just hard and on target. Force the goalkeeper to save it. If far from goal, treat it like a pass and play it quickly before the defence is set. Quick free kicks catch big slow teams off guard.

Make sure they **drink water before and during the match.** On a hard dirt pitch in this heat, dehydration will slow your fast boys down before the second half. Have water ready at the sideline.

Go and beat Adomfe. I want to hear the result.
medaase. if we win I will tell you. if we lose I will also tell you. either way the boys learn something. that is the point. Nyame bless you my friend. good night
That is the right attitude. Win or learn — the boys are lucky to have a coach who sees it that way. Good luck on Saturday. Good night, Kofi.
